cot_prefix: Okay, let's go through the article step by step to find the answer to the question. The question is asking about how the students performed in the experiment, and there are four options provided (A to D). The article describes a study conducted by Dr. Sanda Dolcos and Dr. Dolores. They asked 143 undergraduate students to write down advice to themselves before completing a series of puzzles. Half of the students used the first-person (i.e., "I" statements), while the others used the second-person (i.e., "You" statements). The researchers then observed the students' performance. According to the article, the students who wrote in the second-person "managed to complete more puzzles and appeared to have a more positive attitude." The article also mentions that when the students were asked to write down advice related to exercising more often in the next two weeks, those who used the second-person again reported a more positive attitude and planned to do more exercise than the students who used the first-person. Now, looking at the options: (A) The ones who wrote in the first-person had a better attitude. This contradicts the article, which states that the second-person group had a better attitude, so this is incorrect. (B) The ones who talked to themselves completed the most puzzles. The article doesn't mention talking to themselves; it specifically mentions writing advice to themselves. Also, it says the ones who used the second-person in writing completed more puzzles, so this option is incorrect. (C) The ones who encouraged themselves by writing in the second-person were better. This aligns with the article's findings. The students who used the second-person completed more puzzles and had a more positive attitude. So this is likely
